sentences.text: We will introduce-- will, will-- these are my three grandchildren, most, most favorite grandchildren in America.
We have three grandchildren also in Israel.
But I call them favorite because we love them all equally.
And the, the first one is Sheera.
Her name is Sheera Borenstein.
These are the Borenstein family.
And then is [?
Estee, ?] [?
Estee ?] Borenstein and Yossi Borenstein.
Sheera goes already to high school.
She's a very good student.
And we are very proud of her.
The same with [?
Estee, ?] who is also doing very well in school.
She's, she's going to sixth, what grade is it?
Seventh.
Seventh grade.
And Yossi is going to the second grade.
He's a very good student.
He, he, he studies karate.
He's going to be a fighter.
And also chess-- he's beating me in chess.
He's a good chess player.
So I'm very-- I'm very proud of him, too.
So these are my grandchildren.
And this is the savta.
And I'm the saba.
OK, introduce your wife, please.
My wife, Betty Jacoby, my loving partner, which I'm very proud of her that she loves me.
And I love her.
And we are very happy.
Yeah, I'm very proud of both of my grandparents equally.
And I love them very much.
I'm very proud of how they're so active in remembering the Holocaust and all that they've done.
And I'd like to thank them for always being there for us whenever we need them, and for being so great to us and loving us.
And I love them very much.
Well, my sister pretty much covered it up.
But we also-- we really, really love them.
And we thank them a lot.
And we're really proud of both of them, including my grandfather.
He also, I remember-- I think it was last year he added something to the Haggadah for Passover about the Holocaust then.
He's really active in it.
So we're real proud.
And my savta, she's a very good cook and--
I really love my grandmother because she's a very good cook.
And I really love her.
And I love my saba because he's a good chess player.
And I-- and I really like him a lot.
What should I say?
I-- [CRYING]
She is with emotions.
She cannot speak.
Well, I wish them health and success in life.
And I hope that they will not experience all these things, which I went through in my life when I was their age.
They will have a happy life in America.
And, and they will be happy.
Well, the message to be good children.
They will be good students and proud Jewish people, who will-- who will lead a very good life and be happy.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Well, I, I went back to Czechoslovakia because basically, I wanted to look around-- look after my brothers and, and parents.
Maybe somebody survived the Holocaust.
As I met many people on my way-- on my way to Italy and Italy itself, this-- the people who survived the Holocaust turned-- were hanging around in different places.
And they-- and And I spoke to them.
And some of them told me that they met somebody in Czechoslovakia by the name of Charlie Jacobovitz.
So, so I was wondering if there is another Charlie Jacobovitz?
I have to go to Czechoslovakia.
So I reached-- I got to Prague.
I hung out-- I hung in-- out in Prague, where I can find some information, Red Cross and all these offices.
And filled out forms-- I'm looking for my brother, Charlie Jacobovitz or any other Jacobovitz.
And then they asked me if want to go to work because I needed money.
They wouldn't keep me there for long.
They keep me there in a hotel for about two, three weeks.
Very nice-- they, they, they, they took care of me.
No, that was the Czech government.
The government, who-- we were-- we were supposed to be like repatriates.
We came after the war, coming back to our homeland, to our country back home.
So they were taking care of us.
They, they provided us jobs and, and where to live at, to sleep, and where to live.
So from Prague, they give me a address in Nachod to work in a-- in a factory.
And first-- I mean, in a factory of, of rubber.
They, they manufactured tires and all kind of rubber stuff.
And there, I was earning very good wages.
But it was very, also-- very-- an unhealthy place to work in this environment of rubber chemical factory.
So there, I, I met in the factory a young lady by the name of Marie.
And I got stuck there.
She-- we, we were fond of each other.
And we understood each other very nice.
And she was a nice lady.
And I moved into her apartment.
And we lived together for a while.
So after a year, there's a boy-- she got pregnant.
And a boy was born.
And we named him.
And she was not Jewish.
His name was Jirka, which-- they made it out of-- from my Hebrew name, Yitzhak, they figure that the name is George of English.
And George is-- in Czech is Jirka.
So they named him Jirka.
And since we were not married, he took the name of, of his mother, the family name, Jirka Kuchar, K-U-C-H-A-R.
So I lived-- it was in '48 he was born.
And in-- and in '49, he was already a year old.
I took care of him.
I, I raised him.
I helped Marie to take care of him and everything.
So in '49-- in '48, broke out the war in Israel, in Palestine.
Israel was declared an independent state.
And Arabs attacked the country.
And Israel was in a very-- state of emergency because America wouldn't supply arms for, for the Jewish state to defend themselves.
Nobody, no state in the world would supply arms.
Only the, the Czechoslovakians.
There was-- the Secretary of the Communist party was a Jew.
And he secretly--
His name was Slansky, Rudolf Slansky.
His Jewish name was Saltzman.
He was from my-- from my-- from the Carpathian-- from this area where I am from, from Muncazs or from Ungra, somewhere.
And he turned out to be in Moscow during the war.
He was a very able man.
And he-- and they, they-- the Russians put him as the Secretary of the Communist Party of Czechoslovakia.
He was very popular.
But came the war came with-- in Israel, he was the guy who supplied arms secretly, probably with the approval of, of the Russians, too.
He supplied these arms to Israel.
Secretly, because we went illegally to-- we went there without approval of, of the Russians.
So--
Yeah, so I go there-- I got there, to Israel, with this Haganah.
And when I got there in Israel, the, the war didn't stop, but was a cease-fire.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wrote back to this Marie that she, she want to join me, she can come.
But she said, no, there is a war.
I found another boy who wants to marry me with this-- with this kid-- with the child.
And she didn't come.
She didn't join me.
So in the meantime, I, I served in the Israeli Army.
And then I-- when I was-- I left the Army.
I joined the Israeli police force.
In the police force, I met Betty.
She came as a, a new immigrant from Romania in this town where I served as a policeman.
There we met.
And we married.
We married.
And we had two kids-- two girls, one boy who, who died after one day in the hospital.
He died.
He passed away.
So these two girls and my wife, we moved to another town near Tel Aviv.
And we-- in Tel Aviv, we-- I worked.
I left the Israeli Army-- the Israeli police.
And I worked as a bookkeeper in the huge-- in the biggest supply, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] supply company called [NON-ENGLISH].
So there, in [NON-ENGLISH], I was also-- took part-- while I was working in the police, I served also in another war in 1956.
They call it the Sinai War.
It lasted also a few weeks.
Then in 1967, I served in the Six Day War as an officer of communication.

When I was trained to be a [NON-ENGLISH]-- communication officer in the police, working on the-- on the-- on the-- on the radio as a radio operator-- signals and decoding and keeping up the communications between all the-- all the-- all the other police stations and patrolling the, the streets of Jerusalem and Tel Aviv.
So I was-- at that time, I was serving in the Six Day War-- the Six Day War.
After the Six Day War was over-- and serving as an officer-- it came 1979, when my cousin from Los Angeles came to visit Israel.
And he said to me, come and-- you are having a hard time here to making ends meet.
I will send you a ticket.
Come and take a look at America.
You're American.
Your father was American citizen.
Why not come and see America?
So I came as a visitor.
I got from my job in, in [NON-ENGLISH], I got-- I got permission to visit America and then-- for six months without pay.
I could leave.
I would leave my job.
So I promised to come back in six months.
But after six months I was in America, I was-- I managed to make money and pay off my mortgage, which I was-- it was skyrocketing, my mortgage of the house.
In six months, which I couldn't-- I bought this house in, in-- 10 years before.
And the more I paid my mortgage, the more my, my, debt-- my balance was up.
Because it was-- it was attached to the-- to the index of inflation.
So since the inflation went up, my payments also increased.
And since my wages didn't increase as the inflation did, I was also in, in a deficit.
And I couldn't make it.
So this way, when I make dollars, I, I turned the dollars into Israel money.
It paid off in one shot.
I could paid it off in a few months all my-- all my mortgage free.
And my house was paid off.
So I was very happy.
I said, hey, this is a-- here a-- this is a goldmine here in America.
Why should I go back?
So-- my Betty-- I called Betty.
Should I come back, or what?
She said, no, don't come.
Here it is very hard, hard in Israel.
I want to come and join you.
We'll both work.
We'll make money.
And we'll be better off.
And then, later, maybe, we will go back.
This is what happened.
We sold the house over there.
We bought a house here.
And--
When I came, I worked first as a-- in Long Beach, there was the senior citizen's homes.
I worked as a companion.
And this is also a story-- a long story, I don't want to-- I'll make the story short.
This, this old man, very nice man, I took care of him for almost a year.
And when he died-- he died-- it was also an unfortunate thing that a nurse came.
And she, instead of giving one shot of, of, of this-- he had this diabetes.
So he got shots of how do you call this?
Insulin.
Insulin.
Instead of one cc, she gave him two cc's of insulin.
And he got into a shock.
And he died.
And he, he was 94 years old.
And he had five sons.
And they didn't-- the, the sons was not shocked when he died.
So he's 94 years, so--
And for me, it was very, very suspicious that they were not even taking-- having a post-mortem, you know, this test for him to do it-- what is the reason that he died?
Maybe he did-- this was on purpose done by this nurse, right?
She was not supposed to give only one cc.
The double, double cc of insulin took him into a-- into a shock.
And he died.
So then-- What did you do after that?
--after this, I went, I found-- I had money, a little money saved.
And I was looking.
And I found a senior-citizen home in Long Island, two buildings with about a hundred senior citizens.
And I bought this home.
But I didn't have enough money to pay off the down payment, which was $100,000 he required.
The price was $500,000.
So I was looking for partners.
I found another three partners.
They lend me the money.
And I took them in.

But they were the, the-- at the-- later on, what happened a year later-- we were supposed to invest another $100,000 to install this lift-- elevator and smoke detectors, all kind of things, that cost a lot of money.
And I didn't have any money.
And at that time, my daughter was getting married.
I needed money for her wedding.
So I sold out my share of this home.
And I made the wedding for my daughter.
And with the rest of the money, I, I bought a medallion for Yellow Cab in, in, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Manhattan, in New York City.
So and the-- since then, I, I was working on the Yellow Cab.
I was doing not bad.
And then my, my wife got sick with cancer.
And I had to leave the job.
I had to retire in order to take care of my wife.
So this way-- since then, I've been taking care of my wife.
And, and, and this way I managed to, to help her to survive this, this Holocaust, personal Holocaust, which we are in already since 1985.
We are in such a tragedy.
She's been going to the-- she had four cancer surgeries.
And we were fighting nail and tooth to, to get the best doctors.
I mean, we didn't have the, the, the the medical coverage for these doctors.
We managed to get these doctors, like Dr.
Hirshaut ?] and other doctors, who are the best.
And they don't take-- we didn't have all this insurance coverage.
We didn't have the money.
Somehow, we-- my daughter-- our daughter helped us to get all these doctors.
And she got the best medication and treatment.
OK, my first daughter in America is Rachel.
She's married to Gabriel Borenstein.
And the other daughter's name is Nahama.
She's married to an Israeli boy by the name of Cohen, [?
Jakob ?] Cohen.
Today she is Nahama Cohen.
And they are both very happy.
And they're trying to make it.
Nahama's grandchildren are, the, the first was [?
Maya, ?] a girl; and then second one is [?
Liat, ?] also a girl.
And the third one is a boy by the name of Oren.
So she has three children.
She is very happy.
Especially this boy-- she is so happy that she has this boy with blue eyes.
And that she couldn't-- she couldn't over it when she had him, when she saw what a nice boy she has.
So they are happy.
And he's doing well, now, in Israel.
They are both doing well.
And they are happy.
OK, in Israel, I was-- I belonged to an organization that arranged once a year-- we met-- like, the-- those who are from, from a certain area, they met together, right?
And, and-- like when you go to a school, and you met your friends from school.
How do you call--
A reunion.
We had this reunion every year.
We get together.
And we, we, we, we-- they-- we give money to, to have this in, in such a place like a-- like a Jewish synagogue, put on the wall names of those who, who, who died from, from our town.
And we exchange stories.
And over there, I met from our town-- once we had this reunion, we went to Yad Vashem.
In Jerusalem, there is a museum, Holocaust museum, by the name of Yad Vashem.
And there, I met many, many people from not from our town-- not only from our town, from the surrounding villages, too.
So there was a doctor in our town by the name of Davidovitch.
He treated me when I was sick.
And he know-- he knew my family and my parents.
And he was also-- he was also deported to this Kamenets-Podolskiy.
And there was another lady, which I didn't know her there or then.
She told me she was from Bustino.
I didn't never saw her.
But she says, you were too young boy.
You don't remember me.
And she-- and the-- And these two people, Davidovitch and this lady, is telling me two contradicting stories.
Because Davidovitch is telling me the whole story how it-- how it started.
And also, [?
Bela's ?] father-- Zelinger was his name.
I met him, also.
And he told me a different story also.
Zelinger was the most reliable and credible person because he was our neighbor.
And he went-- they took him, with my father, on the same train.
The other two which told me the stories, they were not taken by the-- at the same train.
There were different transports, deportations on different dates.
Davidovitch said that he heard from somebody that my parents were in this place called [NON-ENGLISH] in a-- in a ghetto.
And from [NON-ENGLISH], they took them on march over to Kamenets-Podolskiy.
And then, no-- he didn't ever hear from them any more.
This woman, also this was hearsays only.
She told me that she heard from somebody else that my father-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] allegedly, he committed suicide.
She heard this.
So that's all what she-- she did no other information, no details, no whatever happened.
That's all what she heard about my father.
About my mother, she knows nothing.
And my brothers, she knows nothing.
This Zelinger, he told me what happened.
He told me, first, how this deportation happened.
In Bustino, he says, they had the list of people.
They went from house to-- where, where the-- where the list says, the dates.
And they told them to pack up.
In one hour, they have to be at the train.
They gave them a certain time.
My father said, no way.
He, he grabbed something there in his hand.
I don't know what he grabbed, a heavy thing.
And he came to this officer and said, listen, you're not going to tell me to go from here.
Or you kill me here-- or I kill you.
This officer, with, with, with this-- with this-- not a handgun, this-- he had the such a big gun, a shotgun.
He hit my father over his head.
My father ran out.
And he destroyed-- he opened the-- they had, in this-- in this-- in the basement, he had drums of oil and of-- and of gasoline, I mean, this petrol.
And all kinds of things which he had, he destroyed.
And he break everything.
And he-- everything, he destroyed almost everything.
And he was getting hit with this-- the policeman was shooting at him.
But he didn't kill him.
So finally, he got calmed down.
And he went to this-- to this-- he piped up.
And my mother cried.
And the kids were crying, asking him, Dad, don't do this.
Let's go.
So they went.
My brother, Charlie, was carrying on his back, the luggage.
And he was very strong.
And all the-- two, two pieces of luggage he was carrying, one on the back, one in his hand.
And they get to the train station over there.
And they were together in the same train with my father.
And Zelinger's there-- told my father, come and let's, let's get away.
Let's-- we, we can-- you can save your life.
And he says, no, I'm not going to leave my wife with, with two kids.
Either we die all together, or we, we go-- we, we, we escape all together.
This Zelinger, he, he went with this train.
And they reached the border of Czechoslovakia and Poland on a town-- this is the, the last stop of the train-- Jasina, they call it.
In Jasina, they get off the train.
And they loaded them on a truck, on trucks.
And this truck take them-- took them to certain villages over there.
And he-- in this village, he, Zelinger, escaped.
He ran away, left his wife there and his daughter.
And he escaped in the woods.
And he-- by-- on foot, he, he managed to get back to Budapest.
And in Budapest, I met him-- not in Israel.
I'm sorry.
I'm met him in Budapest.
And he told me this story in Budapest-- what happened.
And he says, that was the last time I saw your father and your mother together.
They went.
They took them from this village to Kamenets-Podolskiy.
What happened on the way to Kamenets-Podolskiy, he doesn't know.
But there were other stories, which I heard from people from when I sold him these food stamps.
They managed to survive in-- some of them survived from the-- from the pit from where they were killed.
So from them, I heard stories what happened, how they had to cross this river, [NON-ENGLISH], and-- on, on a-- on a bridge.
And they are shooting on them.
And they jumped into the water.
And they-- and they drowned in the water.
Some of them managed to, to swim across or swim away, and then to escape from this water.
OK, we're going to pause now.
Yeah.
